cards.title: Eliminating a single point of failure
cards.when: Any single component's failure takes down the whole system

options.why: A live copy on separate hardware can be promoted on failure. The standard first move for databases.

options.use: Active-active pair behind a load balancer
options.why: Both nodes serve traffic, so failover is instant — but only works for stateless or conflict-free workloads.

cards.tradeoff: Failover speed vs consistency complexity: active-active removes the promotion delay but invites write conflicts.
cards.interviewerProbe: How do you detect that the leader is actually dead and not just slow?

cards.title: Synchronous vs asynchronous replication
cards.when: You must decide whether acknowledged writes may ever be lost

options.why: The write is on a replica before the client gets an ack — leader loss loses nothing.
Lowest write latency and the leader never blocks on a slow replica — but recent writes can vanish in failover.
Caps the durability risk at one lost replica while keeping latency bounded.

cards.tradeoff: Durability vs write latency — pick per data class, not per system.
cards.interviewerProbe: What happens to writes that were in flight when failover started?

cards.title: Automatic vs manual failover
cards.when: The primary fails and something must promote a replica

options.why: Recovery in seconds without paging a human; election prevents two nodes claiming leadership.
A human confirms the primary is truly dead — slower, but immune to false positives that cause split brain.

cards.tradeoff: Recovery time vs split-brain risk: bad automatic failover is worse than slow manual failover.
cards.interviewerProbe: After a network partition heals, how do you prevent the old leader from accepting writes?

cards.title: Scaling reads: replicas vs cache
cards.when: Read traffic is overwhelming the primary database

options.why: Serves the full query surface (any SQL) with modest staleness; scales linearly by adding replicas.
Orders-of-magnitude cheaper per read for hot, repeated lookups — but only for keyable access patterns.

cards.tradeoff: Query flexibility vs cost per read; both introduce staleness you must budget explicitly.
cards.interviewerProbe: Which of your read paths can tolerate stale data, and how stale exactly?

cards.title: Multi-region: active-active vs active-passive
cards.when: Users on multiple continents need low latency and region-failure survival

options.why: No write conflicts ever; remote users pay write latency but reads stay local via replicas.
Local write latency everywhere and no failover event — at the price of conflict resolution machinery.

cards.tradeoff: Write latency for remote users vs the permanent complexity of resolving concurrent cross-region writes.
cards.interviewerProbe: Two regions update the same row in the same second — walk me through what happens.

cards.title: Strong vs eventual consistency
cards.when: Choosing the consistency guarantee for an operation

options.why: Reads always see the latest write — required for balances, inventory, permissions, anything with an invariant.
Higher availability and lower latency — fine for feeds, counters, profiles, where brief disagreement is invisible.

cards.tradeoff: Correctness guarantees vs availability and latency — decide per operation, never per system.
cards.interviewerProbe: Which specific endpoints in your design actually need strong consistency, and why?

cards.title: When you need distributed consensus
cards.when: A cluster must agree on one value: who is leader, a config, a lock

options.why: Battle-tested quorum machinery; you inherit correct leader election and fencing instead of reinventing it.
A single strongly-consistent store with conditional writes (compare-and-set) covers simple locking without new infrastructure.

cards.tradeoff: Operational surface vs correctness under partitions — hand-rolled coordination is where outages are born.
cards.interviewerProbe: With 5 nodes, what is your quorum size, and why is an odd count better?

cards.title: Resolving concurrent writes
cards.when: Two writers can update the same data without seeing each other (multi-leader, offline clients)

options.why: Trivial to implement — but silently discards one write, and clock skew decides the winner.
Data types that merge automatically and deterministically — perfect for counters, sets, collaborative editing.
Surface both versions and let domain logic (or the user) decide — the only option when losing either write is unacceptable.

cards.tradeoff: Silent data loss vs merge complexity.
cards.interviewerProbe: Your servers' clocks drift by 200ms — what does LWW do to your data?

cards.title: Read-your-own-writes
cards.when: A user updates something and must immediately see their change, but reads hit stale replicas

options.use: Route the author's reads to the leader
options.why: Simple session rule: after a write, that user reads from the primary for a short window.
Optimistically render the submitted change locally — no backend change, but divergence shows if the write actually failed.

cards.tradeoff: Backend routing complexity vs trusting the client to fake it honestly.
cards.interviewerProbe: Does your session guarantee hold when the user has two tabs, or a phone and a laptop?

cards.title: Choosing behavior during a partition
cards.when: A network partition happens (it will) and each side must decide what to do

options.use: CP: refuse writes on the minority side
options.why: No divergence to repair afterwards — correct for money, inventory, uniqueness guarantees.

options.use: AP: keep accepting writes, reconcile later
options.why: Users never see an outage — correct for carts, likes, telemetry, where merging later is tolerable.

cards.tradeoff: Visible unavailability now vs reconciliation work (and possible conflicts) later.
cards.interviewerProbe: For each write path in your design, which side of this line is it on?

cards.title: Transactions across services
cards.when: One business action must update multiple services or databases atomically

options.why: Each step commits locally; failures trigger compensating actions. No global locks, works at scale.
True atomicity — but the coordinator is a blocking single point of failure, so keep it to rare, short, intra-datacenter cases.

cards.tradeoff: Designing compensation logic for every step vs blocking the world on a coordinator.
cards.interviewerProbe: What happens when a compensation itself fails?

cards.title: Picking a load-balancing algorithm
cards.when: Distributing requests across a pool of servers

options.why: Dead simple and fine when requests and servers are homogeneous.
Adapts when request costs vary wildly — slow requests stop attracting more traffic.
Same key lands on the same server — required when servers hold per-key state or local cache.

cards.tradeoff: Uniform spread vs affinity: hashing gives locality but makes hot keys hit one server.
cards.interviewerProbe: One instance type in the pool is twice as slow — which algorithm degrades worst?

cards.title: L4 vs L7 load balancing
cards.when: Choosing at which layer the balancer operates

options.why: Routes on path, headers, cookies; terminates TLS; enables canaries and per-route policies.
Higher throughput, lower latency, protocol-agnostic — right for raw TCP or extreme scale tiers.

cards.tradeoff: Routing intelligence vs raw performance; many real systems stack L4 in front of L7.
cards.interviewerProbe: Where does TLS terminate in your design, and what can inspect the traffic after that point?

cards.title: Sticky sessions vs stateless servers
cards.when: Servers hold per-user session state and any server might receive the next request

options.why: Sessions live in Redis or a signed token; any server handles any request, deploys and scale-in are painless.
Zero refactoring — the LB pins users to servers. Acceptable as a bridge, not a destination.

cards.tradeoff: One more infrastructure dependency vs uneven load and sessions dying with their server.
cards.interviewerProbe: What do your users experience during a rolling deploy under sticky sessions?

cards.title: Active vs passive health checks
cards.when: The balancer must stop routing to unhealthy instances

options.why: Dedicated probes on an interval — predictable detection time, and a /health endpoint you control.
Watches real request failures — catches problems synthetic probes miss, with zero probe traffic.

cards.tradeoff: Detection latency vs false positives; production-grade setups usually run both.
cards.interviewerProbe: An instance flaps healthy/unhealthy every few seconds — how do you stop it thrashing the pool?

cards.title: Rate limiting strategy
cards.when: Protecting the system from abusive or bursty clients

options.why: Allows short bursts while enforcing an average rate — matches how legitimate clients actually behave.
Smooth, precise limits with tiny memory cost — better when bursts themselves are the problem.

cards.tradeoff: Burst friendliness vs strictness; also decide WHERE it runs — at the edge (cheap, coarse) or in the service (rich context).
cards.interviewerProbe: Your limiter runs on 20 gateway nodes — how do you enforce one global limit per user?

cards.title: Horizontal vs vertical scaling
cards.when: Current capacity is exhausted

options.why: No practical ceiling and failure isolation for free — but demands stateless services or partitioned state.
Zero code changes and no distribution complexity — buy time with it, but the ceiling and the SPOF remain.

cards.tradeoff: Architectural work now vs a hard limit later.
cards.interviewerProbe: What is the first stateful thing that breaks when you go from 1 server to 10?

cards.title: Cache-aside vs read-through
cards.when: Reads dominate and some staleness is tolerable

options.why: The app checks cache, falls back to DB, fills cache. Simple, cache failure degrades gracefully to the DB.
The cache itself loads misses — centralizes loading logic and dedupes concurrent misses, but adds a library/proxy dependency.

cards.tradeoff: Application-owned simplicity vs centralized miss handling.
cards.interviewerProbe: What hits your database in the first minute after you flush the cache during peak traffic?

cards.title: Write-through vs write-back
cards.when: Writes must interact with a cache that also serves reads

options.why: Write cache and DB together — cache is never stale for written keys, and durability is the DB's.
Ack after writing cache only, flush to DB later — absorbs write bursts, but a cache crash loses acknowledged writes.
Write DB only, let reads repopulate — avoids polluting the cache with write-once data.

cards.tradeoff: Durability vs write latency; write-back is the only one that can lose data.
cards.interviewerProbe: Which of your data classes could survive losing 30 seconds of acknowledged writes?

cards.title: TTL vs event-driven invalidation
cards.when: Cached data changes at the source and staleness must be bounded

options.why: Zero plumbing and self-healing — staleness is bounded by the TTL you choose.
Near-zero staleness — the writer (or CDC stream) evicts affected keys — at the cost of real invalidation plumbing.

cards.tradeoff: Bounded-but-guaranteed staleness vs freshness with machinery that can itself fail.
cards.interviewerProbe: For each cached entity, what is the maximum staleness the product actually tolerates?

cards.title: Preventing cache stampedes
cards.when: A hot key expires and thousands of concurrent requests hit the origin at once

options.why: One request rebuilds the value; the rest wait for it — origin sees one miss instead of thousands.
Serve the stale value while refreshing in the background — users never wait on the rebuild at all.

cards.tradeoff: A brief latency spike for waiters vs serving knowingly stale data during refresh.
cards.interviewerProbe: You deploy and the cache starts empty — how is that different from one hot key expiring, and what breaks?

cards.title: Where to cache: CDN vs app cache vs database
cards.when: Deciding at which layer a caching problem should be solved

options.why: Closest to the user, absorbs traffic before it touches you — for static and anonymously-shared content.
Full control, per-user data, complex keys — the workhorse for computed and personalized results.
Speeds every consumer of the same query without new infrastructure.

cards.tradeoff: Distance from the user vs control over invalidation and personalization.
cards.interviewerProbe: Which layers can safely cache a logged-in user's home feed, and why?

cards.title: Choosing an eviction policy
cards.when: The cache is memory-bound and must discard entries

options.why: Recency is the best default predictor of re-access; cheap and everywhere.
Protects stable hot items from being evicted by one-off bursts — better for skewed, steady workloads.

cards.tradeoff: Adaptation speed vs burst resistance: LRU follows shifts fast but a batch scan can flush your entire hot set.
cards.interviewerProbe: A nightly analytics job reads every record once — what does that do to your LRU cache, and how do you defend it?

cards.title: Work queue vs pub/sub
cards.when: Decoupling producers from consumers

options.why: Each message processed by exactly one worker — for jobs that must happen once (emails, payments, renders).
Every subscriber gets every event — for broadcasting facts that multiple systems independently react to.

cards.tradeoff: Task distribution vs event broadcast — mixing them up is a classic design smell.
cards.interviewerProbe: A subscriber is down for an hour — what happens to its events in each model?

cards.title: Delivery guarantees and idempotency
cards.when: Deciding how the system behaves when message delivery is uncertain

options.why: The industry answer: accept duplicates, make processing safe to repeat via idempotency keys or dedupe.
Fire and forget — acceptable only when losing a message is cheaper than any dedupe machinery (metrics, telemetry).

cards.tradeoff: "Exactly-once delivery" is a myth over networks — what exists is at-least-once delivery plus exactly-once processing you build yourself.
cards.interviewerProbe: Where does your deduplication state live, and how long do you keep it?

cards.title: Preserving event order
cards.when: Events for the same entity must apply in order (account movements, state transitions)

options.use: Partitioned log keyed by entity id
options.why: Order within a partition is guaranteed while partitions process in parallel — order where it matters, throughput everywhere else.
Trivial global order — and a hard throughput ceiling. Fine for low-volume streams.

cards.tradeoff: Parallelism vs ordering scope; global order and horizontal scale are fundamentally at odds.
cards.interviewerProbe: One customer generates 100× the events of everyone else — what happens to their partition?

cards.title: Handling producers outpacing consumers
cards.when: The queue is growing faster than workers drain it

options.why: Fail fast at the edge — callers get an honest error now instead of a timeout after infinite lag.
Absorb bursts and add workers on queue depth — right when the backlog is temporary and work must not be dropped.

cards.tradeoff: Dropping work visibly vs unbounded lag and memory quietly.
cards.interviewerProbe: What is your maximum acceptable queue lag in seconds, and what enforces it?

cards.title: Synchronous call vs async job
cards.when: The caller doesn't strictly need the result in the same request

options.why: Absorbs spikes, isolates downstream failures, retries invisibly — the response just says "accepted".
Simplest correct thing when the caller genuinely needs the answer now; errors surface immediately.

cards.tradeoff: Resilience and elasticity vs immediate confirmation and a simpler mental model.
cards.interviewerProbe: The job is accepted and fails 5 minutes later — how does the client ever find out?

cards.title: Retries and dead-letter queues
cards.when: Message processing fails and might succeed later — or might never

options.use: Bounded retries with backoff, then DLQ
options.why: Transient failures self-heal; permanent failures land somewhere visible instead of poisoning the queue.
Only for messages that must eventually succeed and whose blockage you actively monitor — otherwise one poison message halts the world.

cards.tradeoff: Giving up visibly vs blocking silently.
cards.interviewerProbe: Who looks at the DLQ, how are they alerted, and what's the replay procedure?

cards.title: Relational vs NoSQL as the primary store
cards.when: Choosing the system of record for a new service

options.why: Transactions, joins, constraints, ad-hoc queries — the safest default until a specific limit is hit.
Horizontal write scale and flexible schema — when access is by key, volume is huge, and joins are rare.

cards.tradeoff: Query power and integrity vs effortless scale-out; the mistake is choosing NoSQL for scale you don't have.
cards.interviewerProbe: List the top 3 queries your product needs — which ones become impossible after you shard?

cards.title: Hash vs range partitioning
cards.when: Data outgrows one node and must be split by a key

options.why: Uniform spread by construction — hot spots require a hot key, not just adjacent keys.
Efficient range scans (time windows, prefixes) — but sequential keys pile onto the newest shard.

cards.tradeoff: Uniform load vs scan locality; your dominant query pattern decides.
cards.interviewerProbe: Your key is a timestamp — which partition receives 100% of tonight's writes?

cards.title: Secondary index vs denormalization
cards.when: A read pattern is too slow against the current layout

options.why: Cheapest fix inside one database — at the cost of extra write amplification per index.
Precompute the read shape — unbeatable read latency, but now two copies must be kept consistent.

cards.tradeoff: Write cost on the same table vs consistency responsibility across copies.
cards.interviewerProbe: This table takes heavy writes — what does each added index do to your p99 write latency?

cards.title: LSM trees vs B-trees
cards.when: Choosing a storage engine and your workload skews strongly one way

options.why: Turns random writes into sequential ones — superb write throughput; reads may check several levels.
Predictable point-read latency and mature transactional behavior — the default for read-heavy OLTP.

cards.tradeoff: Write amplification vs read amplification — plus LSM compaction competing with your traffic for I/O.
cards.interviewerProbe: What happens to your p99 read latency while a major compaction is running?

cards.title: Files: object storage vs database blobs
cards.when: Storing images, videos, or documents alongside structured data

options.use: Object storage + URL/key in the DB
options.why: Purpose-built durability and throughput, CDN-friendly, keeps the database small and fast.
One transaction covers data and file — defensible only for small, rarely-read files where atomicity matters most.

cards.tradeoff: Two systems to keep consistent vs one system doing a job it's bad at.
cards.interviewerProbe: A row is deleted but its S3 object survives — how do you prevent, detect, or clean up orphans?

cards.title: Surviving a hot partition
cards.when: One shard receives disproportionate traffic (a celebrity, a viral item)

options.why: Spread one logical key over N physical partitions (key#1..key#N) and merge on read.
Detect outliers and serve them from a dedicated cache or their own capacity — leaves the general scheme untouched.

cards.tradeoff: Permanent read-side merge complexity vs special-case machinery that must detect heat quickly.
cards.interviewerProbe: How do you detect a partition going hot before it becomes an incident?

cards.title: WebSockets vs SSE vs polling
cards.when: The server must push updates to connected clients

options.why: Full duplex — the answer when clients also send frequently (chat, games, collaborative editing).
One-way push over plain HTTP with built-in auto-reconnect — simpler ops for feeds, tickers, notifications.
Works through every proxy and firewall with zero stateful infrastructure — fine when seconds of delay are acceptable.

cards.tradeoff: Bidirectional power vs operational simplicity — and at scale, a million idle sockets is its own architecture problem.
cards.interviewerProbe: A client reconnects after 30 seconds offline — how does it receive the events it missed?

cards.title: Timeouts, retries, and backoff
cards.when: Calling a dependency that sometimes fails or hangs

options.use: Timeout + retries with exponential backoff and jitter
options.why: Transient failures vanish; jitter prevents synchronized retry waves from hammering a recovering service.
Mandatory for non-idempotent operations (payments!) unless there's an idempotency key — a retried charge is an incident.

cards.tradeoff: Availability through persistence vs amplifying load on something already struggling.
cards.interviewerProbe: Every service retries 3×, five layers deep — how many requests hit the bottom service per user click?

cards.title: gRPC vs REST between services
cards.when: Choosing the protocol for service-to-service communication

options.why: Typed contracts, streaming, HTTP/2 multiplexing, less bandwidth — strong for high-volume internal calls.
Universal tooling, human-debuggable, cache- and browser-friendly — the default for public APIs and small teams.

cards.tradeoff: Performance and contract rigor vs ubiquity and debuggability.
cards.interviewerProbe: A browser needs to call this API — what does that force on your gRPC choice?

cards.title: When a CDN earns its place
cards.when: Serving static or cacheable content to a geographically spread audience

options.why: Edge latency, massive origin offload, and free DDoS absorption — a near-automatic win for assets and public pages.
Defensible when the audience is single-region or every response is personalized and uncacheable.

cards.tradeoff: Another cache tier to invalidate vs paying latency and egress on every request.
cards.interviewerProbe: You deploy new JS bundles — how do users stop receiving the old version from the edge?

cards.title: Connection pooling and keep-alive
cards.when: High request volume to the same dependency creates connection churn

options.why: Amortizes TCP/TLS handshakes across requests — order-of-magnitude latency and CPU savings at volume.
Zero shared state and no pool tuning — tolerable only at low volume or in serverless runtimes that forbid pooling anyway.

cards.tradeoff: Pool sizing and lifecycle bugs vs handshake tax on every call.
cards.interviewerProbe: The pool is exhausted and requests queue — what failure do users see, and what limits the queue?

cards.title: When you need distributed tracing
cards.when: A request crosses many services and no single log explains its latency

options.why: One trace shows the whole request tree with per-hop timing — the only tool that localizes cross-service latency.
80% of the value with familiar tooling: propagate one ID and grep — the pragmatic first step.

cards.tradeoff: Instrumentation and storage investment vs manual cross-referencing during incidents.
cards.interviewerProbe: At 100k requests/second you can't trace everything — what's your sampling strategy for the requests that matter?

cards.title: Stopping cascading failures
cards.when: One slow dependency is dragging every upstream service down with it

options.why: After enough failures, stop calling and degrade gracefully — the dependency gets air to recover.
Partition threads/connections per dependency so one slow callee can't exhaust shared resources.

cards.tradeoff: Serving degraded results deliberately vs letting saturation choose what fails for you. Use both; timeouts alone just queue the pain.
cards.interviewerProbe: The breaker is open — what exactly do users see, and how does it decide to close again?

cards.title: Workflow: orchestration vs choreography
cards.when: A multi-step business process spans several services

options.why: One place owns the state machine — visibility, retries, and compensation logic are centralized and debuggable.
Loose coupling, no central dependency — but the workflow exists only implicitly, spread across consumers.

cards.tradeoff: A coordination dependency vs a process nobody can see end-to-end.
cards.interviewerProbe: An order is stuck at step 3 of 6 — in each model, how do you find out and resume it?

cards.title: Canary vs blue-green deployment
cards.when: Shipping a risky change to production

options.why: 1% → 10% → 100% with automatic rollback on error or latency regression — blast radius stays tiny.
Two full environments, instant atomic switch and instant rollback — simpler, but all-or-nothing exposure.

cards.tradeoff: Gradual detection with two versions live (schema compatibility!) vs double infrastructure and binary risk.
cards.interviewerProbe: Both versions run simultaneously during the rollout — what does that force on your database migrations?

cards.title: Verifying resilience with chaos
cards.when: Your design claims to survive failures but nothing has ever proven it

options.use: Fault injection in staging / game days
options.why: Kill nodes, add latency, drop packets in a rehearsal — find the surprises before customers do.
The only environment that tells the truth — but only after staging passes, with scoped targets and an instant abort.

cards.tradeoff: Realism vs risk; chaos without a hypothesis and an abort switch is just an outage you scheduled.
cards.interviewerProbe: What's your abort criterion, and who has authority to hit it?
